assumptions: Input length can be recursively factored (especially powers of two).
Complex arithmetic cost model is dominant in runtime analysis.
claims: Recursive factorization drastically reduces arithmetic operation count versus direct DFT.
conclusions: FFT is a practical algorithmic primitive for large transform workloads.
contributions: Formalized recursive decomposition of DFT into smaller DFTs.
Established asymptotic speedup foundation for modern FFT software/hardware.
future_work: Algorithm variants for mixed radices and architecture-aware implementations.
limitations: Operation-count analysis does not model modern cache/memory hierarchy effects.
summary: Introduced the radix-2 divide-and-conquer FFT formulation that reduced DFT complexity from quadratic to near-linearithmic and enabled practical spectral computing.
title: An Algorithm for the Machine Calculation of Complex Fourier Series

assumptions: Planner overhead can be amortized across repeated transform calls.
Microarchitecture-specific tuning materially impacts runtime.
claims: Self-optimizing planning usually outperforms generic/public FFT implementations.
conclusions: Empirical tuning is essential for FFT performance portability.
contributions: Introduced codelets and planning search over decomposition trees.
Demonstrated architecture-adaptive performance gains.
future_work: Broader SIMD and multicore-aware planner strategies.
limitations: Planning time can be high for one-off transforms.
summary: Presented FFTW planning and runtime adaptation, showing superior practical performance over many fixed implementations.
title: FFTW: An Adaptive Software Architecture for the FFT

assumptions: Empirical plan search approximates optimal implementation choice.
Composite-size decomposition dominates real workloads.
claims: Planner-driven approach delivers robust cross-platform performance.
conclusions: Autotuned FFT libraries can remain competitive across architecture generations.
contributions: Systematized planner heuristics and codelet generation.
Documented reproducible benchmarking methodology for FFT libraries.
future_work: More aggressive vectorization and multicore/cluster extensions.
limitations: Planning and codelet generation complexity can limit constrained deployments.
summary: Detailed FFTW3 architecture including planner search space, code generation, and portability techniques for high performance.
title: The Design and Implementation of FFTW3

assumptions: Transform identities can be represented compositionally and searched automatically.
Performance objective can be approximated from generated kernel timings.
claims: Generated code can match or exceed expert-tuned libraries on target platforms.
conclusions: Program generation is viable for FFT portability and sustained optimization.
contributions: Transform formula language + code generation pipeline.
Automatic search over algorithmic and implementation variants.
future_work: Heterogeneous targets and richer performance models.
limitations: Search cost grows rapidly with transform family and hardware options.
summary: Introduced a DSL and search-based synthesis system for architecture-adapted FFT and related transform kernels.
title: SPIRAL: A Generator for Platform-Adapted Libraries of Signal Processing Algorithms

assumptions: Structured transform decompositions expose optimization opportunities.
Search-guided synthesis scales across transform families.
claims: Autogenerated kernels can achieve near-peak performance.
conclusions: Domain-specific generation is effective for sustained transform optimization.
contributions: Unified representation for FFT/DCT/wavelet code generation.
Learning-guided search for implementation selection.
future_work: Runtime adaptation and heterogeneous backends.
limitations: Toolchain complexity and tuning overhead.
summary: Extended SPIRAL into broader DSP transform generation with evidence of competitive performance versus hand-tuned libraries.
title: SPIRAL: Code Generation for DSP Transforms

assumptions: Distributed-memory transpose dominates scaling at high process counts.
Pencil decomposition alleviates slab decomposition limits.
claims: 2D decomposition improves scalability compared with slab methods.
conclusions: Pencil decomposition is preferable for extreme-scale 3D FFT workloads.
contributions: Portable framework for large-scale 3D FFT.
Practical guidance on decomposition and parameter choices.
future_work: Hybrid MPI+threads/GPU extensions.
limitations: Performance still communication-bound at very large scale.
summary: Presented scalable 3D FFT with pencil decomposition and reported strong/weak scaling on large HPC systems.
title: P3DFFT: A Framework for Parallel Computations of Fourier Transforms in Three Dimensions

assumptions: Bulk-synchronous communication model with latency/bandwidth terms.
Global transpose cost dominates at scale.
claims: Communication, not flops, is the limiting factor at extreme scale.
conclusions: Algorithmic choices must target communication reduction first.
contributions: Communication-centric model for parallel 3D FFT.
Implications for decomposition and network-aware design.
future_work: Topology-aware communication schedules and overlap strategies.
limitations: Model calibration depends on system/network specifics.
summary: Analyzed 3D FFT communication lower bounds and architecture implications for exascale systems.
title: On the Communication Complexity of 3D FFTs and Its Implications for Exascale

assumptions: MPI collectives govern distributed transpose performance.
GPU-CPU and network paths materially affect total FFT runtime.
claims: Collective strategy strongly affects large-scale FFT throughput.
conclusions: Communication-aware library design is mandatory for exascale FFT.
contributions: Empirical analysis on Summit for communication bottlenecks.
Design recommendations for GPU-centric distributed FFT.
future_work: Adaptive collectives and deeper overlap of communication/computation.
limitations: Results tied to tested interconnect and MPI implementation.
summary: Compared collective communication designs for large distributed FFT on multi-GPU systems and motivated heFFTe design choices.
title: Impacts of Multi-GPU MPI Collective Communications on Large FFT Computation

assumptions: 3D FFT can be decomposed into staged 1D FFT + transposes.
Communication dominates at high node counts.
claims: Reported >40x local-kernel gains vs CPU baselines and >2x full FFT gains in tested settings.
conclusions: heFFTe is suitable as a core exascale FFT component.
contributions: Portable C++/CUDA+MPI library for large-scale FFT.
Detailed algorithmic decomposition and communication analysis.
future_work: More backends and deeper communication/computation overlap.
key_equations: N_0/P_{i0}\times N_1/P_{i1}\times N_2/P_{i2}\xrightarrow[]{reshape+1D\ FFTs}\hat N_0/P_{o0}\times\hat N_1/P_{o1}\times\hat N_2/P_{o2}
limitations: Performance remains sensitive to network collective behavior.
summary: Introduced heFFTe for exascale-targeted distributed FFT with strong GPU scalability and communication models.
title: heFFTe: Highly Efficient FFT for Exascale

assumptions: Cross-API implementation can retain near-vendor-level performance.
GPU memory behavior and kernel fusion are central to FFT throughput.
claims: Comparable or better performance than vendor-specific alternatives in tested cases.
conclusions: Open cross-platform FFT can be viable for production HPC workloads.
contributions: Unified open-source FFT backend for Vulkan/CUDA/HIP/OpenCL/Metal.
Precision and performance validation on modern GPUs.
future_work: Broader architecture tuning and distributed extensions.
limitations: Hardware- and size-dependent performance variance remains substantial.
summary: Described VkFFT design and benchmarking across GPU APIs, reporting competitive performance and precision against vendor libraries.
title: VkFFT - A Performant, Cross-Platform and Open-Source GPU FFT Library

assumptions: Runtime code generation can offset heterogeneity costs.
Device selection can be abstracted via runtime scheduler.
claims: System improves portability without manual per-device rewrites.
conclusions: Dynamic generation + runtime orchestration is practical for FFT kernels.
contributions: Linked FFTX codegen and IRIS portability runtime.
Demonstrated heterogeneous execution for FFT-centric kernels.
future_work: Scale-out experiments and broader workload coverage.
limitations: Evidence focused on selected benchmarks and platforms.
summary: Presented integration of FFTX runtime codegen with IRIS runtime for heterogeneous portability and benchmarked small FFT kernels.
title: FFTX-IRIS: Towards Performance Portability and Heterogeneity for SPIRAL Generated Code

assumptions: Kernel-based spreading/interpolation dominates NUFFT cost and error.
No-precompute interfaces are desirable for practical workflows.
claims: Competitive or superior speed/memory behavior for many 3D/high-accuracy cases.
conclusions: FINUFFT is a practical default for CPU NUFFT workloads.
contributions: New kernel with favorable accuracy-speed tradeoff.
Multithreaded implementation and benchmark suite against existing libraries.
future_work: Deeper GPU and distributed-memory support.
limitations: Performance depends on point distribution and tolerance regime.
summary: Introduced FINUFFT with a new spreading kernel and showed high-accuracy/high-throughput NUFFT performance on CPUs.
title: A Parallel Non-uniform Fast Fourier Transform Library Based on an Exponential of Semicircle Kernel

assumptions: Oversampled FFT + interpolation is an efficient NUFFT approximation strategy.
Worst-case error optimization is suitable for kernel parameter tuning.
claims: Min-max interpolation yields lower approximation errors than prior methods.
conclusions: Interpolation design should be driven by explicit approximation criteria.
contributions: Min-max criterion for interpolation design.
Comparative error evaluation versus conventional kernels.
future_work: Extension to higher dimensions and hardware-aware tuning.
limitations: Trade-offs remain between error, kernel width, and runtime.
summary: Proposed min-max interpolation NUFFT design that reduces worst-case approximation error for nonuniform frequency sampling.
title: Nonuniform Fast Fourier Transforms Using Min-Max Interpolation

assumptions: Window-based gridding gives controllable NUFFT error/time tradeoff.
Library users need multiple NUFFT variants under one API.
claims: NFFT3 provides robust and reusable NUFFT routines for scientific computing.
conclusions: Unified software abstraction improves reproducibility for nonequispaced transforms.
contributions: Comprehensive open-source NUFFT library architecture.
Support for multiple transform types and practical parameterization.
future_work: Automatic parameter tuning and accelerator support.
key_equations: f(x_j)=\sum_{k\in I_N} \hat f_k e^{-2\pi i k\cdot x_j}
limitations: Parameter sensitivity can complicate out-of-box optimality.
summary: Described NFFT3 software architecture and algorithms for nonequispaced transforms used in imaging and inverse problems.
title: NFFT 3: A Software Library for Various Nonequispaced Fast Fourier Transforms

assumptions: Low-frequency mode truncation captures dominant operator behavior.
Training data covers target PDE parameter distribution.
claims: Reported strong speed/accuracy tradeoffs and zero-shot super-resolution behavior.
conclusions: Fourier parameterization is effective for global operator learning.
contributions: Resolution-invariant operator-learning architecture based on FFT layers.
Empirical gains on Burgers, Darcy, and Navier-Stokes tasks.
future_work: Better handling of discontinuities and adaptive mode selection.
limitations: Performance degrades when high-frequency modes dominate and are underrepresented.
summary: Introduced Fourier-domain neural operator layers with FFT complexity for learning PDE solution operators across discretizations.
title: Fourier Neural Operator for Parametric Partial Differential Equations

assumptions: Token mixing can be decoupled from learned attention weights for many tasks.
Feed-forward nonlinearity recovers expressivity lost from attention removal.
claims: Substantial training speedups with modest accuracy loss.
conclusions: Fourier mixing is a viable efficiency baseline for long-context models.
contributions: Simple Fourier token mixer architecture.
Compute/accuracy tradeoff results on GLUE/LRA.
future_work: Hybrid attention+Fourier architectures.
limitations: Task-dependent accuracy regressions versus full attention.
summary: Replaced transformer self-attention with unparameterized Fourier token mixing to improve throughput while preserving much of baseline accuracy.
title: FNet: Mixing Tokens with Fourier Transforms

assumptions: Frequency-domain sparsification can preserve task signal while reducing complexity.
Global convolution framing is suitable for token mixing.
claims: Outperforms or matches efficient attention alternatives on tested settings.
conclusions: Operator-learning ideas transfer effectively to vision token mixing.
contributions: AFNO block with linear memory scaling in sequence length.
High-resolution segmentation results with efficient token mixing.
future_work: Automatic sparsity scheduling and broader modality evaluation.
limitations: Performance depends on mode sparsification hyperparameters.
summary: Adapted Fourier operator layers for vision transformers using block-diagonal channel mixing and frequency sparsification.
title: Adaptive Fourier Neural Operators: Efficient Token Mixers for Transformers

assumptions: Long-range dependencies can be captured by structured convolutions.
FFT acceleration is practical for large-context sequence models.
claims: Convolutional alternatives can compete with transformer baselines in certain regimes.
conclusions: FFT-enabled convolution is a strong primitive for long sequence modeling.
contributions: Hyena operator stack with efficient long-convolution design.
Language modeling results at long context lengths.
future_work: Improved kernel parameterization and systems-level optimization.
limitations: Kernel design/training stability can be sensitive for very long contexts.
summary: Proposed long-convolution language model hierarchy where FFT-based convolutions are central for long-context efficiency.
title: Hyena Hierarchy: Towards Larger Convolutional Language Models

assumptions: Structured matrices can replace dense attention-like mixing with better hardware throughput.
claims: Higher throughput and competitive quality at long sequence lengths.
conclusions: Structured mixers are viable for efficient large-context models.
contributions: Unified sub-quadratic primitive across sequence and channel dimensions.
Reported throughput and quality tradeoffs across NLP/CV settings.
future_work: Integration with faster convolution/FFT kernels.
limitations: Task-dependent tuning and architecture-specific kernels required.
summary: Presented sub-quadratic structured mixing architecture; relevant as downstream consumer of fast Fourier/conv primitives in long-sequence systems.
title: Monarch Mixer: A Simple Sub-Quadratic GEMM-Based Architecture

assumptions: FFT conv bottlenecks are dominated by tensor-core underutilization and memory I/O.
Block-skipping sparsity can preserve quality for long-sequence tasks.
claims: Reported up to 7.93x kernel speedup and significant end-to-end gains in tested models.
conclusions: Systems-level kernel design can substantially improve practical FFT-conv efficiency.
contributions: Tensor-core FFT decomposition with fused kernels.
Partial and frequency-sparse convolution algorithms.
future_work: Wider architecture coverage and auto-tuning of sparse patterns.
limitations: Requires hardware-specific kernel engineering and memory constraints.
summary: Optimized FFT-based long convolutions with tensor-core-friendly decomposition and sparse variants, reporting large speedups over baseline FFT conv.
title: FlashFFTConv: Efficient Convolutions for Long Sequences with Tensor Cores

assumptions: Repeatable runtime and round-trip error metrics can rank FFT implementations.
claims: cuFFT was fastest on tested GTX Tesla/Titan configurations.
conclusions: Automated benchmark tooling is necessary for fair FFT library selection.
contributions: Cross-library benchmarking utility and empirical comparisons on NVIDIA GPUs.
future_work: Broader device coverage and reproducible benchmark standardization.
limitations: Hardware scope limited to tested devices and library versions.
summary: Proposed a benchmarking application for comparing FFT library performance and precision across available CPU/GPU devices.
title: Benchmark for FFT Libraries

assumptions: Algorithmic taxonomy helps compare practical FFT variants.
claims: FFT progress depends on both arithmetic complexity and implementation concerns.
conclusions: No single FFT variant dominates all workload/hardware cases.
contributions: Comprehensive review of Cooley-Tukey, split-radix, prime-factor, and Winograd methods.
future_work: Architecture-specific benchmarking and automated selection methods.
limitations: Predates modern multicore/GPU memory hierarchy realities.
summary: Surveyed major FFT families and implementation issues, framing open problems around algorithmic complexity and practical deployment.
title: Fast Fourier Transforms: A Tutorial Review and a State of the Art

assumptions: Parallel communication mapping materially changes realized FFT speedup.
claims: Alternative data mappings can reduce communication and improve flexibility.
conclusions: Parallel FFT design must co-optimize decomposition and data motion.
contributions: Unified treatment of FFT parallel variants.
Communication complexity discussion for multiprocessors.
future_work: Model updates for hierarchical and heterogeneous systems.
limitations: Legacy architecture assumptions relative to modern accelerators.
summary: Compared FFT parallelization approaches in a unified framework and introduced additional communication-aware variants.
title: FFT Algorithms and Their Adaptation to Parallel Processing

assumptions: FFTW-MPI slab decomposition limits can be overcome with multidimensional decomposition.
claims: Improves scalability for large distributed FFT workloads.
conclusions: FFTW-compatible extensions aid adoption in HPC codes.
contributions: Practical interface similar to FFTW-MPI with extended decomposition support.
Open-source implementation and tests.
future_work: Hybrid MPI/OpenMP improvements and wider backend support.
limitations: Depends on external FFTW and MPI stack quality.
summary: Presented PFFT as a massively parallel extension to FFTW with multidimensional data decomposition for distributed-memory systems.
title: PFFT - An Extension of FFTW to Massively Parallel Architectures

claims: Supports broad transform modes and precisions with optimized kernels.
conclusions: Primary source for runtime capabilities/constraints in NVIDIA environments.
contributions: Operational API specification for reproducible cuFFT benchmarking.
limitations: Does not provide third-party comparative fairness criteria.
summary: Vendor reference for CUDA FFT APIs, supported transform types, precision modes, plan behavior, and multi-GPU features.
title: cuFFT 13.2 Documentation

claims: Fast and accurate HIP-based 1D/2D/3D FFT support for GPU devices.
conclusions: Canonical source for AMD GPU FFT benchmark setup and feature checks.
contributions: Defines supported transform features and operational guidance for ROCm benchmarking.
limitations: No vendor-neutral comparison methodology.
summary: Official ROCm FFT documentation detailing supported dimensions/types, APIs, and distributed/runtime compilation workflows.
title: rocFFT Documentation (ROCm 7.2.0)

claims: Enumerates supported real/complex 1D/2D/3D transforms by target device.
conclusions: Useful for constraining benchmark suites to portable transform subsets.
contributions: Capability matrix needed for benchmark feature parity checks across libraries.
limitations: Performance guidance is not comprehensive for all workloads.
summary: Reference matrix for oneMKL DFT capabilities across CPU/GPU and dimensionality/transform-type support.
title: Intel oneMKL DFT Functionality Reference

claims: Documents specific 1/2/3D FFT optimization updates on Intel GPU families.
conclusions: Benchmark reports should record exact oneMKL release due to frequent optimization changes.
contributions: Provides date-stamped optimization changes to avoid stale benchmark assumptions.
limitations: Release notes are descriptive, not full methodology/performance proofs.
summary: Release notes with recent DFT optimizations and supported hardware details relevant for recency-aware benchmark planning.
title: Intel oneMKL Release Notes

claims: Highlights adaptive planning strategy and linked publications.
conclusions: Required canonical source when citing FFTW software provenance.
contributions: Primary project-level source for FFTW references and distribution.
limitations: Not a peer-reviewed performance evaluation itself.
summary: Canonical project site for FFTW releases, papers, and implementation notes.
title: FFTW Home Page

claims: Contains canonical source distribution snapshots and version history.
conclusions: Essential for code-level reproducibility in benchmark harnesses.
contributions: Provides inspectable source for reproducibility and integration checks.
limitations: Repository mirror warning notes special checkout/release process.
summary: Source repository for FFTW3 build system, code, and maintenance history.
title: FFTW GitHub Repository

claims: Cross-platform backend supports Vulkan/CUDA/HIP/OpenCL/Metal workflows.
conclusions: High-reuse codebase candidate for benchmark inclusion.
contributions: Open-source implementation tied to IEEE Access VkFFT paper.
limitations: Performance portability still depends on backend-specific tuning.
summary: Official VkFFT implementation repository covering multiple GPU APIs and integration examples.
title: VkFFT GitHub Repository

claims: Targets scalable heterogeneous FFT on HPC platforms.
conclusions: Strong candidate for distributed benchmark baselines.
contributions: Open-source distributed FFT implementation aligned with ICCS heFFTe paper.
limitations: Requires MPI/HPC environment for full feature validation.
summary: Reference implementation of distributed exascale FFT workflows and backend integrations (FFTW, MKL, cuFFT, rocFFT, etc.).
title: heFFTe GitHub Repository

claims: Supports type 1/2/3 NUFFT in 1D/2D/3D with precision control.
conclusions: Useful for expanding benchmark scope beyond uniform FFT.
contributions: Production-quality NUFFT code aligned with SIAM SISC paper.
limitations: Separate project needed for GPU acceleration coverage.
summary: Maintained NUFFT implementation with multi-language bindings and benchmark tooling for CPU workflows.
title: FINUFFT GitHub Repository

claims: Provides CUDA-accelerated NUFFT operations compatible with FINUFFT ecosystem.
conclusions: Enables GPU extension of transform benchmark matrix.
contributions: GPU-side NUFFT baseline for high-throughput nonuniform workloads.
limitations: CUDA-only; cross-vendor portability is limited.
summary: GPU NUFFT implementation for type-1/type-2 transforms in 1D/2D/3D on CUDA platforms.
title: cuFINUFFT GitHub Repository

claims: Supports architecture-aware transform kernel synthesis.
conclusions: Important reusable candidate for auto-tuned benchmark baselines.
contributions: Reusable code generation pipeline for transform specialization.
limitations: Higher onboarding complexity than prebuilt libraries.
summary: Public source repository for SPIRAL program generation framework used to synthesize optimized FFT and related transform code.
title: SPIRAL Software GitHub Repository

assumptions: Communication overhead dominates strong-scaling behavior in standard 3D FFT pipelines.
Point-to-point exchange patterns can outperform all-to-all on some fat-tree deployments despite higher arithmetic cost.
claims: Alternative communication pattern can improve scalability in selected network regimes.
Communication characteristics can outweigh extra arithmetic complexity.
conclusions: Communication-aware algorithm selection is environment-dependent; FFT is not universally optimal at scale.
contributions: Introduces S3DFT distributed algorithm and implementation.
Provides scaling analysis on fat-tree networked clusters and contrasts communication behavior with parallel FFT.
future_work: Broader topology studies and hybrid strategy selection between FFT and tensor-multiplication DFT.
limitations: Comparisons are tied to tested cluster/network topologies and implementation choices.
summary: Presents S3DFT, an alternative distributed 3D DFT method that reduces all-to-all usage by employing point-to-point communication with modified Cannon-style tensor-matrix multiplication.
title: 3D DFT by Block Tensor-Matrix Multiplication via a Modified Cannon's Algorithm: Implementation and Scaling on Distributed-Memory Clusters with Fat Tree Networks

assumptions: Pseudo-spectral Navier-Stokes solvers are FFT-bound at extreme resolutions.
OpenMP target offloading plus GPU kernels can sustain exascale throughput with careful memory/communication design.
claims: Frontier-scale runs show feasible extreme-resolution turbulence with GPU-first Fourier methods.
conclusions: FFT-centric pseudo-spectral algorithms remain viable at exascale with architecture-aware implementation.
contributions: Demonstrates production-scale FFT-heavy simulation up to tens of trillions of grid points.
Documents practical systems choices for exascale GPU pseudo-spectral pipelines.
future_work: Portability evaluation across additional exascale systems and communication/runtime tuning layers.
limitations: Results are tied to Frontier hardware/software stack and turbulence workload specifics.
summary: Reports exascale Fourier pseudo-spectral turbulence simulations on Frontier and characterizes performance constraints and scaling for FFT-intensive workloads.
title: GPU-enabled Extreme-Scale Turbulence Simulations: Fourier Pseudo-Spectral Algorithms at the Exascale using OpenMP Offloading

assumptions: Adder architecture is a dominant factor in FPGA FFT energy-delay outcomes.
Split-radix decomposition can improve resource/performance balance versus radix-2 and radix-4 in tested hardware flow.
claims: Split-radix configuration with proposed adder provides favorable speed-power-area tradeoff in tested setup.
conclusions: Hardware-level arithmetic design materially changes FFT implementation ranking.
contributions: Introduces and evaluates a new parallel-prefix adder in multiple FFT structures.
Provides comparative power, area, and frequency results across radix variants.
future_work: ASIC evaluations and broader precision/size exploration.
limitations: Findings depend on selected FPGA toolchain/process and implementation settings.
summary: Compares radix-2/radix-4/split-radix hardware implementations using a proposed parallel-prefix adder to reduce power-delay and improve speed.
title: Optimized Power and Speed of Split-Radix, Radix-4 and Radix-2 FFT Structures

assumptions: Matrix-core intrinsic utilization and memory-traffic reduction are primary levers for multidimensional GPU FFT speedups.
Stockham formulation maps effectively to matrix-multiplication style kernels.
claims: Reports average performance gains for 1D/2D/3D FFT and large 3D advantage versus compared libraries on tested platform.
conclusions: Matrix-core-oriented design can improve practical GPU FFT throughput for multidimensional workloads.
contributions: Introduces register-level elementwise multiplication scheduling and shared-memory reordering for FFT.
Benchmarks against rocFFT and VkFFT on AMD MI250.
future_work: Cross-platform validation and automated tuning for matrix-core FFT kernels.
limitations: Evaluation is platform-specific and may not generalize across GPU architectures.
summary: Proposes matrix-core-based Stockham FFT optimization on AMD MI250, reporting improved 1D/2D/3D performance over rocFFT and VkFFT in tested cases.
title: Design and Optimization of High-Performance Multi-Dimensional FFT Based on Matrix Core

assumptions: Conventional FFT mappings are suboptimal on NPU dataflow constraints.
Heterogeneous CPU+NPU scheduling can improve energy-performance tradeoffs for transform preprocessing.
claims: NPU-aware transform strategy improves practical throughput/efficiency over naive mappings in tested scenarios.
conclusions: Hardware-specific transform mapping is essential for edge SoC deployments.
contributions: Adapts Fourier computation strategy to NPU execution characteristics.
Evaluates performance/efficiency implications in edge-oriented contexts.
future_work: Broader NPU families and mixed-precision numerical fidelity analysis.
limitations: Generalization beyond studied NPU stack and application mix is limited.
summary: Investigates Fourier-transform acceleration strategies tailored to NPU-oriented heterogeneous SoCs with energy constraints for wearable/edge contexts.
title: Research on High-Performance Fourier Transform Algorithms Based on the NPU

assumptions: Communication dominates at scale and can be reduced with lower-precision exchange while preserving overall accuracy through normalization.
Mixed-precision strategy is acceptable for target application error tolerances.
claims: Reported faster execution than double-precision baseline and stronger performance than selected open-source baselines on tested systems.
conclusions: Mixed-precision communication is a practical lever for large-scale FFT efficiency.
contributions: Introduces shared-exponent communication compression for distributed FFT.
Combines kernel, memory, and message optimizations in a large-scale GPU framework.
future_work: Adaptive precision control and broader architecture portability analysis.
limitations: Accuracy-speed tradeoff depends on workload spectra and tolerance requirements.
summary: Presents mixed-precision large-scale distributed FFT with shared-exponent communication compression and two-phase normalization, showing strong scaling to 4096 GPUs.
title: MFFT: A GPU Accelerated Highly Efficient Mixed-Precision Large-Scale FFT Framework

assumptions: All-to-all and point-to-point communication tuning strongly impacts end-to-end FFT scaling.
Model-guided tuning can improve application-level performance for FFT-heavy workloads.
claims: Careful tuning can achieve near-linear scaling regimes and notable acceleration for FFT-dependent applications.
conclusions: Communication-model-guided parameter tuning is necessary for practical large GPU FFT deployments.
contributions: Introduces a parameterized algorithm including grid shrinking and batched transforms.
Develops bandwidth-centric performance analysis on large multi-GPU systems.
future_work: Extending tuning strategies to new exascale GPU architectures and libraries.
limitations: Measured outcomes are tied to Summit/Spock-era hardware and software stacks.
summary: Analyzes multi-GPU multidimensional FFT performance with bandwidth/communication models and tuning across Summit/Spock systems.
title: Performance Analysis of Parallel FFT on Large Multi-GPU Systems
